This problem is known as the "sliding window" coverage problem. For example, for each window of length L, we need to select at least one element to modify. The minimal number of elements to modify is the minimal number of points to hit all the windows.

This is known as the "hitting set" problem for intervals, which is NP-hard in general. However, for the case of intervals that are consecutive and overlapping, there's a greedy approach that can be applied.

The greedy approach for hitting set for intervals is to select the earliest possible end of the current interval. For example, when processing intervals in order, whenever you find an interval not yet covered, you select the end of that interval as a point to hit, which covers as many subsequent intervals as possible.

Let me think about the following: For a subarray to be stable, all elements must have a common divisor ≥2. To break it, we can modify at least one element in it. The minimal number of changes required to break all such subarrays is the minimal number of elements to modify such that every subarray longer than L has at least one modified element.

This is equivalent to the problem of placing the minimal number of "markers" such that every subarray of length > L has at least one marker. This is similar to the problem of placing guards in a line to cover all long intervals.

The optimal way to do this is to place markers at positions that cover as many long subarrays as possible. For example, for subarrays of length L+1, placing a marker at the end of each such subarray can cover multiple overlapping subarrays.

This brings us back to the earlier idea of using a greedy approach for the hitting set problem for intervals of length L+1.

So, for a given L, we need to process all windows of length L+1 that are currently stable (i.e., their GCD is ≥2). For each such window, we need to ensure that at least one element is modified. The minimal number of modifications is computed using the greedy hitting set approach.

But how to compute which windows are currently stable?

This brings us back to the problem of efficiently computing the GCD for all windows of length M (L+1).

Let me think about how to compute the GCD for all windows of length M efficiently.

An efficient way to compute the GCD for all sliding windows of size M is possible using a deque-like approach. Here's an idea inspired by the sliding window minimum problem:

For each window, we can maintain a data structure that keeps track of the GCDs of the elements in the window. When a new element is added to the window, we can compute the GCD of the new element with the current GCD of the window. However, this approach works only if the window is built incrementally. But when elements are removed from the front, this approach doesn't work because the GCD of the remaining elements is not easily computable.

For example, consider a window [a, b, c]. The GCD is G. If we remove a, the new window is [b, c], and the GCD is GCD(b, c), which is not directly derivable from G.

This suggests that there's no straightforward way to maintain the GCD for a sliding window efficiently. Hence, for each window of size M, we would need to compute the GCD from scratch, which takes O(M) time per window. For n windows, this would be O(n*M) time, which is not feasible for large M and n.

Given that n is up to 1e5, and M can be up to n, this approach would be O(n^2), which is not feasible.

This suggests that the initial approach is not viable for large inputs. We need a different idea.

Let me think about the problem from another angle. What if we focus on the fact that modifying an element to 1 will break any subarray it is part of? Since 1 has a GCD of 1 with any other number.

So, the optimal way to break a subarray is to change one of its elements to 1. This ensures that the entire subarray's GCD becomes 1.

Thus, for any stable subarray, modifying one element to 1 will break it. So, the problem reduces to selecting the minimal number of elements to modify to 1 such that every stable subarray of length > L is broken.
